title: 34M withdrawing $50k from company shares
body:
O.P.
Hi all,
I'm a 34-year-old male looking for thoughts on the best way to handle a $50k withdrawal from long-held company shares of 32 years ($25k this FY and $25k next FY). I am not seeing any further potential long term growth in the company so I want to diversify my portfolio and given the CGT discount being discontinued as part of the reasons for withdrawing. I'll have about $15k left in the company shares that I want to keep as a buffer for emergencies or life changes and I don't plan to touch the $50k.
My situation: 1. Bought an investment property 5 years ago with decent equity built up 2. PPOR has ~$160k left on the loan (variable rate around current market levels) 3. Already voluntarily contributing 7.75% of income to super on top of employer SG (15.4%) 4. Long-term goal (retirement/wealth building) – comfortable with growth assets but want some flexibility (I recently revised my super and moved over to hostplus to 'indexed high growth' with lower fees). 5. Particularly interested in low-fee Vanguard ETFs but feeling overwhelmed by the options (VAS, VGS, VDHG etc.)
I'm torn between: 1. Putting more into super (concessional contributions) 2. Investing in ETFs outside super for accessibility 3. Using some to pay down the PPOR loan by leaving it in the offset account.
Questions: 1. Given my age and long horizon, should I prioritise maxing concessional super contributions with part of this money, or focus more on ETFs outside super? 2. For a simple Vanguard ETF approach, is something like VDHG the way to go, or a VAS + VGS mix? Any other low-fee options worth considering? Should I also contributing toward the ETF on a fortnightly/monthly basis? 3. How much (if any) of the $50k would you put toward the PPOR/offset vs investing, considering current interest rates? 4. Any other angles I should be thinking about with the company share withdrawal (tax timing across FYs, overall portfolio diversification etc.)?
Appreciate any balanced thoughts or experiences from people in similar situations. I know this isn't personalised advice and I'll be speaking to a professional as well.
